title: Three-Shelf Rolling Storage Cart
summary: A general-purpose rolling cart with three plywood-bottomed shelves framed in 1x3 on 2x4 ladder legs — a plain utility cart, distinct from this catalog's lumber rack, bar cart, and outfeed table.
description:
Rip a single plywood sheet into three 15-3/4"-wide strips to become the shelf bottoms — do this with a table saw fence set to 15-3/4", or with a circular saw measuring and cutting one line at a time rather than marking all three cuts first, since the blade's own width shifts each successive measurement. The 2x4 legs and cross pieces form two ladder-style end frames, assembled with self-tapping screws or pocket holes if you have a jig.

Build each shelf as its own small 1x3 frame first, then glue the bottom edges and nail the plywood panel to the underside of each frame — a framed-and-bottomed shelf, not a bare plywood panel resting on cleats. **Place the finished shelves inside the ladder frame and screw down through both the shelf and into the legs** — this is what actually locks the whole cart together, not just the ladder frames on their own.

Casters go on last, and this build works equally well with light-duty 1/4" plywood shelf bottoms or heavier 1/2" plywood if you plan to load it with anything substantial.

materials.name: Caster wheels, 2 in, with mounting screws

steps.title: Cut the parts
steps.body: Rip the 4 ft x 4 ft plywood into three shelf bottoms, each 15-3/4" wide by 48" long. Three at 15-3/4" is 47-1/4" of the 48" you have, so the two saw kerfs consume almost all the remainder — measure and cut one line at a time off the freshly cut edge rather than marking all three up front, because the blade's own width shifts every measurement made after the first cut. Then cut the framing: from the 8 ft 2x4s, four legs at 33" and six shelf supports at 17-1/4" (two legs and one support come off each of two boards, and the remaining four supports off the third). From the 6 ft 1x3s, six shelf sides at 49-1/2" and six shelf ends at 15-3/4" — one of each per board, which uses all six boards exactly. Square every cut and label the pieces; there is no spare stock anywhere in this list to fall back on.

steps.title: Build the ladder frames
steps.body: Build two identical ladder-style end frames, each from two 33" legs and three 17-1/4" shelf supports running horizontally between them, one support per shelf level. The 17-1/4" length is exactly the outside width of a finished shelf frame, which tells you the shelves drop down between the legs and land on top of these supports rather than hanging off their ends. The source doesn't set the three shelf heights along the 33" legs — pick them for what you'll actually store — but stand both leg pairs side by side and mark all three heights across all four legs at once, so the shelves come out level with each other rather than each frame merely being right on its own. Fasten with 2-1/2" self-tapping screws through the legs into the ends of each support, or with pocket holes if you have a jig, and check each frame square across its diagonals.

steps.title: Build the shelf frames
steps.body: Build each of the three shelf frames from two 49-1/2" shelf sides and two 15-3/4" shelf ends: the 49-1/2" sides run the full outside length and the 15-3/4" ends fit between them, giving a frame 49-1/2" long by 17-1/4" wide once you add 3/4" of side at each end. Nail and glue, screw, or pocket-hole the corners, checking the diagonals before the glue sets. Then run glue along the frame's bottom edges and nail a 15-3/4" x 48" plywood panel to the underside with 1-1/4" finish nails — 48" is exactly the frame's inside length (49-1/2" less two 3/4" ends) and 15-3/4" its inside width, so the panel lands inset 3/4" from the frame's outside edges all round. That even inset is your check: if the panel overhangs anywhere, two parts have been swapped.

steps.title: Install the shelves
steps.body: Set each shelf frame down between the two ladder frames, resting on its pair of 17-1/4" supports, and drive 2" wood screws down through the shelf frame's sides into the supports and the legs. This is the step that actually locks the cart together — the two ladder frames on their own are just two flat panels, and it is the shelves tying them to each other that makes the whole thing rigid, which is why the shelves are screwed rather than dropped in loose. Twelve 2" screws is one at each of the twelve shelf-to-leg junctions, so place each one where the shelf frame overlaps solid leg. Check the cart doesn't rock and the shelves are level before you flip it for the casters.

steps.title: Add the wheels
steps.body: Turn the cart upside down and screw a 2" caster to the bottom of each 33" leg. A 2x4 leg presents a 1-1/2" x 3-1/2" pad, which is enough for a small caster plate but not by much — position each plate so all its screws land on the leg, and back any that hang over with a scrap block screwed up under the bottom shelf. Set the cart down and check it doesn't rock; a rocking cart on four fresh casters is almost always one leg cut long, not a bad wheel.
materials: Caster wheels, 2 in, with mounting screws

steps.title: Finish (optional)
steps.body: Sand the 1x3 shelf frames and the plywood before finishing, easing the top outside edges of each shelf frame in particular — those are the edges your knuckles meet when you reach into a loaded shelf. Stain or paint if you want it. A shop cart works perfectly well unfinished, but any film finish means spilled glue and finish scrape off later instead of soaking into bare wood.
